title: Read picture graphs (multi-step problems)
description: Interpret picture graphs to answer questions about a context.

assessment_items.question_content:
The picture graph below shows the flowers in Ori's garden over several months.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

**How many fewer flowers did Ori have in her garden in March and July combined than in May?**     

[[☃ input-number 1]] flowers
hints: Each [[☃ image 1]]equals  $\maroonD3$ flowers.

We need to count how many pictures are over each month on the picture graph.  

[[☃ image 1]]

There are $\blueD2$ pictures over March.

$\blueD2 \times \maroonD3 = \blueD{6}$

Ori had $\blueD6$ flowers in her garden in March.

We also need to know how many flower Ori had in July and May.

[[☃ image 1]]

Month | Number of[[☃ image 2]]| Number of flowers
:- | :-: | :-
$\blueD{\text{March}}$ | $2$ | $\maroonD3\times 2= \blueD6$
$\purpleD{\text{May}}$ | $7$ | $\maroonD3\times 7 = \purpleD{21}$  
$\goldE{\text{July}}$ | $3$ | $\maroonD3\times 3 = \goldE{9}$

First, we add to see how many flowers are in Ori's garden in $\blueD{\text{March}}$ and $\goldE{\text{July}}$ combined.

$\blueD {6} + \goldE{9} = {15}$

Next, we subtract to see how many fewer ${15}$ flowers is than the number of flowers in $\purpleD{\text{May}}$.

$\purpleD{21} - {15} = 6$
Ori's garden had $6$ fewer flowers in  March and July combined than in May.

options.alt: A flower picture equals 3 flowers.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Months and the vertical axis labeled Flowers in Ori’s garden. Each month is listed along the horizontal axis from the left of the axis to the right of the axis as follows: March, April, May, June, and July. The number of flowers in Ori’s garden for each month is represented by the number of flower pictures plotted on the graph. March is represented by 2 flower pictures, April is represented by 5 flower pictures, May is represented by 7 flower pictures, June is represented by 6 flower pictures, and July is represented by 3 flower pictures.

assessment_items.question_content:
The picture graph below shows the number of squirrels participating in different dance classes.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

**How many more squirrels are in tap and ballet combined than in hip hop?**   

[[☃ input-number 1]] squirrels
hints: Each [[☃ image 1]] equals  $\greenD5$ squirrels.

We need to count how many pictures are over each class on the picture graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are $\blueD3$ pictures over tap.

$\blueD3\times \greenD5 = \blueD{15}$

There are $\blueD{15}$ squirrels in tap classes.

We also need to know how many squirrels are in ballet and hip hop.

[[☃ image 1]]

Dance class| Number of [[☃ image 2]]| Number of squirrels
:- | :-: | :-:
$\blueD{\text{Tap}}$ | $3$ | $3\times\greenD5= \blueD{15}$  
$\purpleD{\text{Ballet}}$ | $4$ | $4\times\greenD5 = \purpleD{20}$
$\goldE{\text {Hip hop}}$ | $5$ | $5\times\greenD5=\goldE{25}$

First, we add to see how many squirrels are in $\blueD{\text{tap}}$ and $\purpleD{\text{ballet}}$ combined.

$\blueD {15} + \purpleD {20} = \maroonD{35}$

Next, we subtract to see how many more $\maroonD{35}$ squirrels is than the number of squirrels in $\goldE{\text{hip hop}}$.

$\maroonD{35} - \goldE{25} = 10$
There are $10$ more squirrels in tap and ballet combined than in hip hop.

options.alt: A squirrel picture equals 5 squirrels.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Dance classes and the vertical axis labeled Squirrels. Each dance class is listed along the horizontal axis from the left of the axis to the right of the axis as follows: tap, ballet, jazz, and hip hop. The number of squirrels for each dance class is represented by the number of squirrel pictures plotted on the graph. Tap is represented by 3 squirrel pictures, ballet is represented by 4 squirrel pictures, jazz is represented by 6 squirrel pictures, and hip hop is represented by 5 squirrel pictures.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mandy's science class counted the bones in an owl pellet.    

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

**How many more rib bones were there than skull and jaw bones combined?** 

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] bones
hints: Each [[☃ image 1]]equals  $\blueD{2}$ bones.

We need to count how many pictures are over each kind of bone on the picture graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

There is $\blueD1$ picture over skull.

$\blueD1\times \greenD2 = \blueD{2}$

There are $\blueD{2}$ skull bones.

We also need to know how many bones are jaw and rib bones. 

[[☃ image 1]]

Kind of bone | Number of [[☃ image 2]] | Number of bones
:- | :-: | -:
$\blueD{\text{Skull}}$ | $1$ | $1\times\greenD2=\blueD{2}$
$\maroonD{\text{Jaw}}$ | $0$ | $0\times\greenD2=\maroonD{0}$
$\purpleD{\text{Rib}}$ | $3$ | $3\times\greenD2=\purpleD{6}$

First, we add to see how many $\blueD{\text{skull}}$ and $\maroonD{\text{jaw}}$ bones combined were in the owl pellet.

$\blueD {2} + \maroonD{0} = \goldE{2}$

Next, we subtract to see how many more $\purpleD{\text{rib}}$ bones were there than the $\goldE{2}$ bones we already counted.

$\purpleD{6} - \goldE{2} = 4$
There were $4$ more rib bones than skull and jaw bones combined.

options.alt: A bone picture.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Number of bones and the vertical axis labeled Kind of bone. Each bone kind is listed along the vertical axis from the bottom of the axis to the top of the axis as follows: shoulder, rib, leg, jaw, and skull. The number of bones for each bone kind is represented by the number of bone pictures plotted on the graph. Shoulder is represented by 1 bone picture, rib is represented by 3 bone pictures, leg is represented by 2 bone pictures, jaw is represented by 0 bone pictures, and skull is represented by 1 bone picture.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ruby had a budget of up to $\$75$ to spend.  Ruby made a graph of the costs of the items she bought.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

**How much money did Ruby have left from her budget?** 

$\$$[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Each 
[[☃ image 1]]

equals  $\purpleD{\$5}$.

We need to count how many pictures are across household items on the picture graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are $\maroonD2$ pictures across from household items. 

$\maroonD2\times\purpleD5 = \maroonD{10}$

Ruby spent $\$\maroonD{10}$ on household items.

Now we need to find how much money was spent on each item.

[[☃ image 1]]

Type of purchase | Number of ![A price tag.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7ad141ae076fe4ffb3f879a49ea8509ebb7f4c48) | Cost
:- | :-: | :-
$\maroonD{\text{Household items}}$ | $2$ | $2\times\$\purpleD{5} =\$\maroonD{10}$
$\blueD{\text{Toys}}$ | $1$ | $1\times\$\purpleD{5}=\$\blueD5$
$\greenD{\text{Food}}$ | $4$ | $4\times\$\purpleD{5} =\$\greenD{20}$
$\redD{\text{Clothing}}$ | $6$ | $6\times\$\purpleD{5} =\$\redD{30}$

Let's add to find out how much Ruby spent.

$\maroonD{10}+\blueD5+\greenD{20}+\redD{30}  = \purpleD{65}$

Ruby had a budget of up to $\$\goldE{75}$.  Let's subtract the amount Ruby spent to see how much she had left.

$\$\goldE{75} - \$\purpleD{65} = \$10$
Ruby had $\$10$ left from her budget.

options.alt: A price tag.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Cost (in dollars) and the vertical axis labeled Type of purchase. Each item is listed along the vertical axis from the bottom of the axis to the top of the axis as follows: Clothing, Food, Toys, and Household items. The cost of each item is represented by the number of prices tags plotted on the graph. Clothing is represented by 6 price tags, Food is represented by 4 price tags, Toys is represented by 1 price tag, and Household Items is represented by 2 price tags.

assessment_items.question_content:
Marie has $50$ pages in her notebook.  She made a graph of the kinds of writing on all of the pages she has used so far.


[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

**How many pages are left in Marie's notebook?** 

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] pages
hints:
Each [[☃ image 1]] 
equals  $\blueD{2}$ pages.

We need to count how many pictures are across each kind of writing on the picture graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are $\redD5$ pictures across from journal. 

$\redD5\times\blueD2 = \redD{10}$

There are $\redD{10}$ journal pages.

Now we need to find the number of pages for each kind of writing.

[[☃ image 1]]

Kind of writing | Number of [[☃ image 2]]| Number of pages
:- | :-: | :-
$\maroonD{\text{Poems}}$ | $2$ | $2\times\blueD{2}=\maroonD4$
$\goldE{\text{Reports}}$ | $3$ | $3\times\blueD{2}=\goldE6$
$\purpleD{\text{Stories}}$ | $6$ | $6\times\blueD{2}=\purpleD{12}$
$\greenD{\text{Jokes}}$ | $3$ | $3\times\blueD{2}=\greenD6$
$\redD{\text{Journal}}$ | $5$ | $5\times\blueD{2}=\redD{10}$

Let's add to find out how many pages Marie has used.

$\maroonD4 + \goldE6 + \purpleD{12} + \greenD6 + \redD{10}  = \tealD{38}$

There are ${50}$ pages in Marie's notebook.  Let's subtract the number of pages Marie has used to see how many she had left.

${50} - \tealD{38} = 12$
Marie has $12$ pages left in her notebook.

options.alt: A page picture.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Number of pages and the vertical axis labeled Kind of writing. Each kind of writing is listed along the vertical axis from the bottom of the axis to the top of the axis as follows: Journal, Jokes, Stories, Reports, and Poems. The number of pages for each kind of writing is represented by the number of pages pictures plotted on the graph. Journal is represented by 5 page pictures, Jokes are represented by 3 page pictures, Stories are represented by 6 page pictures, Reports are represented by 3 page pictures, and Poems are represented by 2 page pictures.

assessment_items.question_content:
Nancy put $80$ acorns in her backyard.  Then, she made a graph of how many acorns different types of squirrels ate.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

**How many acorns were left after the squirrels ate?** 

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Each [[☃ image 1]]
 $=4$ acorns.


So, each[[☃ image 2]]
 $=2$ acorns.

We need to count how many acorns the grounds squirrel ate. 

[[☃ image 1]]

There are $\maroonD{5}$ full pictures and a $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$ picture across from ground squirrel. 
 
$(\maroonD5\times4) + \maroonD2 = \maroonD{22}$

The ground squirrel ate $\maroonD{22}$ acorns.

Now we need to find how many acorns each squirrel ate.

[[☃ image 1]]

Kind of squirrel | Number of [[☃ image 2]] | Number of acorns
:- | :-: | :-
$\maroonD{\text{Ground}}$ | $5\dfrac12$ | $({5} \times4) + 2=\maroonD{22}$
$\blueD{\text{Flying}}$ | $2$ | $2\times4=\blueD8$
$\greenD{\text{Fox}}$ | $6$ | $6\times4 =\greenD{24}$
$\redD{\text{Gray}}$ | $2\dfrac12$ | $(2\times4) + 2 =\redD{10}$

Let's add to find out how many acorns the squirrels ate.

$\maroonD{22} + \blueD8 + \greenD{24} + \redD{10}  = \purpleD{64}$

There were $\goldE{80}$ acorns in Nancy's backyard.  Let's subtract the amount the squirrels ate to find how many were left.

$\goldE{80} - \purpleD{64} = 16$
There were $16$ acorns left in Nancy's backyard.

options.alt: An acorn picture.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Number of acorns and the vertical axis with kind of squirrel. Each kind of squirrel is listed along the vertical axis from the bottom of the axis to the top of the axis as follows: gray squirrels, fox squirrels, flying squirrels, and ground squirrels. The number of acorns for each squirrel is represented by the number of acorn pictures plotted on the graph. Gray squirrel is represented by 2 and 1 half acorn pictures, fox squirrel is represented by 6 acorn pictures, flying squirrel is represented by 2 acorn pictures, and ground squirrel is represented by 5 and 1 half acorn pictures.

assessment_items.question_content:
Olivia counted the number of ladybugs on each plant in her garden, then made the graph below.


[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

**If $10$ ladybugs fly from the lettuce to the alfalfa, which $2$ kinds of plants will have the same number of ladybugs?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each [[☃ image 1]]
equals  $\blueD{5}$ ladybugs.

We need to count how many pictures are above lettuce on the picture graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are $\purpleD3$ pictures above lettuce.

$\purpleD3\times\blueD5 = \purpleD{15}$

There are $\purpleD{15}$ ladybugs on the lettuce plant.

Now we can find how many ladybugs were on each plant. 

[[☃ image 1]]

Kind of plant | Number of [[☃ image 2]] | Number of ladybugs
:- | :-: | :-
$\goldE{\text{Roses}}$ | $7$ | $7\times\blueD{5} =\goldE{35}$
$\purpleD{\text{Lettuce}}$ | $3$ | $3\times\blueD{5} =\purpleD{15}$
$\greenD{\text{Alfalfa}}$ | $5$ | $5\times\blueD{5} =\greenD{25}$
$\maroonD{\text{Grape vines}}$ | $2$ | $2\times\blueD{5} =\maroonD{10}$

Before some of the ladybugs move, there are $\purpleD{15}$ ladybugs on the lettuce.  Then $\maroonD{10}$ ladybugs fly *off of* the lettuce.

$\purpleD{15} - \maroonD{10} = {5}$ ladybugs

After that, there are ${5}$ ladybugs left on the lettuce.  Do any other plants have ${5}$ ladybugs on them?

Before some of the ladybugs move, there are $\greenD{25}$ ladybugs on the alfalfa.  Then $\purpleD{10}$ ladybugs fly *onto* the alfalfa.

$\greenD{25} + \purpleD{10} = \redD{35}$ ladybugs

After that, there are $\redD{35}$ ladybugs on the alfalfa.  Do any other plants have $\redD{35}$ ladybugs on them?
The roses have $\redD{35}$ ladybugs on them.
After the ladybugs fly, the *roses* and *alfalfa* will have the same number of ladybugs on them.

options.alt: A ladybug picture.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Kind of plant and the vertical axis labeled Number of ladybugs. Each plant kind is listed along the horizontal axis from the left of the axis to the right of the axis as follows: roses, lettuce, alfalfa, and grape vines. The number of ladybugs for each plant kind is represented by the number of ladybug pictures plotted on the graph. Roses are represented by 7 ladybug pictures, lettuce is represented by 3 ladybug pictures, alfalfa is represented by 5 ladybug pictures, and grape vines are represented by 2 ladybug pictures.

assessment_items.question_content:
James just moved to a new home.  He made a graph showing  the number of boxes in each room.


[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

James moved $3$ boxes from the living room to the bedroom.


**After James moved those $3$ boxes, which two rooms had the same number of boxes?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Each [[☃ image 1]]equals  $\blueD{3}$ boxes.

We need to count how many pictures are across from the living room.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are $\greenD3$ pictures across from living room.

$\greenD3\times\blueD3 = \greenD{9}$

There are $\greenD{9}$ boxes in the living room.

Now we can find the number of boxes moved in each room.

[[☃ image 1]]

Room | Number of [[☃ image 2]]| Number of boxes
:- | :-: | :-
$\greenD{\text{Living}}$ $\greenD{\text{room}}$ | $3$ | $3\times\blueD{3}=\greenD{9}$
$\purpleD{\text{Kitchen}}$ | $5$ | $5\times\blueD{3} =\purpleD{15}$
$\maroonD{\text{Bedroom}}$ | $4$ | $4\times\blueD{3}=\maroonD{12}$
$\goldE{\text{Bathroom}}$ | $1$ | $1\times\blueD{3} =\goldE3$

Before James moved the boxes, there $\greenD{9}$ boxes in the living room.  Then he moved $\purpleD{3}$ boxes *away*  to the bedroom.

$\greenD{9} - \purpleD{3} = \redD{6}$ boxes

After that, there were $\redD{6}$ boxes in the living room.

Before James moved the boxes, there $\maroonD{12}$ boxes in the bedroom.  Then he moved $\purpleD{3}$ more boxes to the bedroom.

$\maroonD{12} + \purpleD{3} ={15}$ boxes

After that, there were ${15}$ boxes in the bedroom.
Which two rooms have the same number of boxes after these changes?
After James moved the boxes, the **kitchen**  and the **bedroom** had the same number of boxes.

options.alt: A box picture.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Number of boxes and the vertical axis labeled Room. Each room is listed along the vertical axis from the bottom of the axis to the top of the axis as follows: bathroom, bedroom, kitchen, and living room. The number of boxes for each room is represented by the number of box pictures plotted on the graph. Bathroom is represented by 1 box picture, bedroom is represented by 4 box pictures, kitchen is represented by 5 box pictures, and living room is represented by 3 box pictures.

assessment_items.question_content:
Karen picked apples at the Carter Apple Orchard this weekend. She made a graph to show how many apples she picked.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

Karen ate $5$ Fiji apples and dropped $4$ Gala apples.

**After Karen ate and dropped those apples, which two types have the same number of apples?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each [[☃ image 1]] equals $\blueD{2}$ apples.

So, [[☃ image 2]] equals $\blueD1$ apple.

We need to count how many pictures are across from Pink Lady.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are $\maroonD{2}$ full pictures and a $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$ picture across from Pink Lady. 
 
$(\maroonD2\times2) + \maroonD1 = \maroonD{5}$

There are $\maroonD{5}$ Pink Lady apples.

Now we need to find how many apples there were of each kind. 

[[☃ image 1]]

Kind of apple| Number of [[☃ image 2]]| Number of apples
:- | :-: | :-
$\maroonD{\text{Pink Lady}}$ | $2\dfrac12$ | $({2} \times\blueD2) + 1=\maroonD{5}$
$\purpleD{\text{Rome}}$ | $1$ | $1\times\blueD2=\purpleD2$
$\greenD{\text{Fiji}}$ | $6$ | $6\times\blueD2 =\greenD{12}$
$\goldE{\text{Gala}}$ | $3\dfrac12$ | $(3\times\blueD2) + 1 =\goldE{7}$
$\redD{\text{Granny Smith}}$ | $1\dfrac12$ | $(1\times\blueD2) + 1 =\redD{3}$

Before Karen ate any Fiji apples, there were $\greenD{12}$ apples.  Then she ate $5$ apples.

$\greenD{12} -5 = {7}$ apples

After that, there were $\greenD{7}$ Fiji apples.

Before Karen dropped apples, there were $\goldE{7}$ Gala apples. Then she dropped ${4}$ apples.

$\goldE{7} - 4 ={3}$ apples

After that, there were $\goldE{3}$ apples.
Which two types of apples have the same number of apples after these changes?
After Karen ate and dropped apples, the **Gala**  and the **Granny Smith** apples had the same number of apples.

options.alt: An apple picture equals 2 apples.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Number of apples and the vertical axis labeled Apple type. Each apple type is listed along the vertical axis from the bottom of the axis to the top of the axis as follows: Granny Smith, Gala, Fiji, Rome, and Pink Lady. The number of apples for each apple type is represented by the number of apple pictures plotted on the graph. Granny Smith is represented by 1 and 1 half apple pictures, Gala is represented by 3 and 1 half apple pictures, Fiji is represented by 6 apple pictures, Rome is represented by 1 apple picture, and Pink Lady is represented by 2 and 1 half apple pictures.

assessment_items.question_content:
Iris is heading a recycling drive.  The chart below gives the number of kilograms of each kind of recycling brought to the drive.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

**How many kilograms of recycling have been brought to the drive in all?** 

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] kilograms
hints: Each[[☃ image 1]]equals  $\maroonD{3}$ kilograms.

We need to count how many pictures are over each kind of recycling in the picture graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are $\blueD6$ pictures across from Aluminum.

$\blueD6\times \maroonD3 = \blueD{18}$

There were $\blueD{18}$ kilograms of aluminum brought into the recycling center.

Now, we need to know how many kilograms of each kind of recycling were brought in.

[[☃ image 1]]

Kind of recycling | Number of [[☃ image 2]]| Kilograms
:- | :-: | :-
$\goldD{\text{Paper}}$ | $8$ | $8\times\maroonD{3} =\goldD{24}$
$\greenD{\text{Glass}}$ | $2$ | $2\times\maroonD3=\greenD6$
$\purpleD{\text{Plastic}}$ | $4$ | $4\times\maroonD3=\purpleD{12}$
$\blueD{\text{Aluminum}}$ | $6$ | $6\times\maroonD3=\blueD{18}$

Let's add to find out the total number of kilograms.

$\goldD{24} + \greenD6 + \purpleD{12} + \blueD{18}  = {60}$
There  have been $60$ kilograms of recycling brought to the drive.

options.alt: A recycling sign picture.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Kilograms and the vertical axis labeled Kind of recycling. Each recycling kind is listed along the vertical axis from the bottom of the axis to the top of the axis as follows: aluminum, plastic, glass, and paper. The kilograms for each recycling kind are represented by the number of recycling sign pictures plotted on the graph. Aluminum is represented by 6 recycling sign pictures, plastic is represented by 4 recycling sign pictures, glass is represented by 2 recycling sign pictures, and paper is represented by 8 recycling sign pictures.

assessment_items.question_content:
Some students at Derin's school signed a petition asking for a reading area for recess.  The graph below shows the number of signatures from each grade.


[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

**How many total signatures are on the petition?** 

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] signatures
hints: Each [[☃ image 1]]equals  $\purpleD{2}$ signatures.

We need to count how many pictures are across from $1^{\text{st}}$ grade.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are $\blueD3$ pictures across from $1^{\text{st}}$ grade.

$\blueD3\times \purpleD2 = \blueD{6}$

There were $\blueD{6}$ signatures from $1^{\text{st}}$ grade.

Now, we need to know how many signatures were on the petition from each grade.

[[☃ image 1]]

Grade | Number of [[☃ image 2]]| Number of signatures
:- | :-: | :-
$\blueD{1^{\text{st}}}$ | $3$ | $3\times\purpleD{2} =\blueD6$
$\greenD{2^{\text{nd}}}$ | $2$ | $2\times\purpleD{2}=\greenD4$
$\goldE{3^{\text{rd}}}$ | $8$ | $8\times\purpleD{2}=\goldE{16}$
$\maroonD{4^{\text{th}}}$ | $7$ | $7\times\purpleD{2}=\maroonD{14}$

Let's add to find out the total number of signatures.

$\blueD6 + \greenD4 + \goldE{16} + \maroonD{14}  = {40}$
There  are $40$ signatures on the petition.

options.alt: A signature picture.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Number of signatures and the vertical axis labeled School grade. Each school grade is listed along the vertical axis from the bottom of the axis to the top of the axis as follows: fourth, third, second, and first. The number of signatures for each school grade is represented by the number of signature pictures plotted on the graph. Fourth grade is represented by 7 signature pictures, third grade is represented by 8 signature pictures, second grade is represented by 2 signature pictures, and first grade is represented by 3 signature pictures.

assessment_items.question_content:
A grocer makes a graph of how many bananas are sold at different stores on Tuesday.

**How many total bananas did the stores sell on Tuesday?** 

[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]


[[☃ numeric-input 1]] bananas
hints:
Each [[☃ image 1]]equals  $\blueD6$ bananas sold.

So,[[☃ image 2]] equals $\blueD3$ bananas sold.

We need to count how many pictures are across from Fruit Zone.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are $\maroonD{4}$ full pictures and a $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$ picture across from Fruit Zone. 
 
$(\maroonD4\times6) + \maroonD3 = \maroonD{27}$

There are $\maroonD{27}$ bananas at Fruit Zone.

Now we need to find how many bananas were sold at each store.
[[☃ image 1]]

Store | Number of [[☃ image 2]]| Number of bananas sold
:- | :-: | :-:
$\maroonD{\text{Fruit Zone}}$ | ${4\dfrac12}$ | $(4\times\blueD6) + 3= \maroonD{27}$
$\greenD{\text{Grocery Town}}$ | $6$ | $6\times\blueD6= \greenD{36}$
$\goldE{\text{Fab Food}}$ | ${3}$ | $3\times\blueD6= \goldE{18}$  
$\purpleD{\text{Meal Market}}$ | ${2\dfrac12}$ | $(2\times\blueD6) + 3 = \purpleD{15}$

We can add to find the total.

$\maroonD{27} + \greenD{36} + \goldE{18} + \purpleD{15} = 96$ bananas
The stores sold a total of $96$ bananas on Tuesday.

options.alt: A banana picture.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Bananas sold on Tuesday and the vertical axis labeled Store. Each store is listed along the vertical axis from the bottom of the axis to the top of the axis as follows: Meal Market, Fab Food, Grocery Town, and Fruit Zone. The number of bananas sold on Tuesday for each store is represented by the number of banana pictures plotted on the graph. Meal Market is represented by 2 and 1 half banana pictures, Fab Food is represented by 3 banana pictures, Grocery Town is represented by 6 banana pictures, and Fruit Zone is represented by 4 and 1 half banana pictures.
